title: 13_grad_accumulation_scale.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Make Four Micro-Batches Equal One Big Batch

The ranker used to train with batch 256 on a big GPU and converged reliably. After the move to smaller hardware that only fits 64 rows, the trainer was refactored to gradient accumulation: four micro-batches per optimizer step, `optimizer.step()` and `zero_grad()` once per window. The contract is exact — one accumulation window must produce the same gradient as one full-batch pass over the same 256 rows.

Since the refactor, runs diverge inside the first epoch with hyperparameters that the large-batch baseline handles fine. This notebook is the parity harness the team uses to settle the argument: on identical weights it takes one full-batch gradient as the reference, replays one accumulation window exactly as the production loop does, and asserts the gradient norms agree. The gate is red — and the ratio it prints is suspiciously round. Work out which line breaks the equivalence.
initialCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

torch.manual_seed(0)
X = torch.randn(256, 32)
y = torch.randn(256, 1)
accum_steps = 4

model = nn.Linear(32, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

loss_fn(model(X), y).backward()
base_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.zero_grad()

for xb, yb in zip(X.chunk(accum_steps), y.chunk(accum_steps)):
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    loss.backward()
accum_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()

ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"
traceback:
full-batch grad norm 1.2970 | accumulated 5.1881 | ratio 4.0000
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 26
     24 ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
     25 print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
---> 26 assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"

AssertionError: parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = 4.0000 (expected ~1.0)

options.label: Divide the learning rate by accum_steps to compensate for the larger accumulated gradient
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

torch.manual_seed(0)
X = torch.randn(256, 32)
y = torch.randn(256, 1)
accum_steps = 4

model = nn.Linear(32, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1 / accum_steps)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

loss_fn(model(X), y).backward()
base_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.zero_grad()

for xb, yb in zip(X.chunk(accum_steps), y.chunk(accum_steps)):
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    loss.backward()
accum_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()

ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"
options.resultLog:
full-batch grad norm 1.2970 | accumulated 5.1881 | ratio 4.0000
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 26
     24 ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
     25 print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
---> 26 assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"

AssertionError: parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = 4.0000 (expected ~1.0)
options.rationale: The learning rate never touches `.grad`, so the harness fails with the identical 4.0000 — and the harness is right to. For plain SGD a 4× gradient times lr/4 happens to land on the same update, but the moment anything else consumes the gradient the disguise collapses: norm-based clipping clamps against a 4× inflated norm, gradient logging and anomaly alerts read 4× values, and switching to Adam (whose update is largely scale-invariant in the gradient but not in lr) turns your compensation into a genuine 4× under-step. Fix the quantity, not the consumer.

options.label: Move optimizer.zero_grad() to the top of the micro-batch loop so gradients cannot pile up
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

torch.manual_seed(0)
X = torch.randn(256, 32)
y = torch.randn(256, 1)
accum_steps = 4

model = nn.Linear(32, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

loss_fn(model(X), y).backward()
base_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.zero_grad()

for xb, yb in zip(X.chunk(accum_steps), y.chunk(accum_steps)):
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    loss.backward()
accum_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.step()

ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"
options.resultLog:
full-batch grad norm 1.2970 | accumulated 1.5456 | ratio 1.1916
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
AssertionError                            Traceback (most recent call last)
Cell In[1], line 26
     24 ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
     25 print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
---> 26 assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"

AssertionError: parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = 1.1916 (expected ~1.0)
options.rationale: This restores the familiar zero → forward → backward rhythm and the explosion goes away — but so does the accumulation. Zeroing per micro-batch discards the first three gradients, so `step()` sees only the last 64 rows: you are now doing small-batch training with three wasted forward/backward passes per step. The 1.1916 ratio is the sampling noise of a quarter-size batch, not a scaling constant, which is exactly the signature the parity harness exists to catch. Accumulation means zeroing once per *window*, never per micro-batch.

options.label: Scale each micro-batch loss by 1/accum_steps before backward(), keeping step and zero_grad once per window
options.patchCode:
import torch
from torch import nn

torch.manual_seed(0)
X = torch.randn(256, 32)
y = torch.randn(256, 1)
accum_steps = 4

model = nn.Linear(32, 1)
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1)
loss_fn = nn.MSELoss()

loss_fn(model(X), y).backward()
base_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.zero_grad()

for xb, yb in zip(X.chunk(accum_steps), y.chunk(accum_steps)):
    loss = loss_fn(model(xb), yb)
    (loss / accum_steps).backward()
accum_norm = model.weight.grad.norm().item()
optimizer.step()
optimizer.zero_grad()

ratio = accum_norm / base_norm
print(f"full-batch grad norm {base_norm:.4f} | accumulated {accum_norm:.4f} | ratio {ratio:.4f}")
assert abs(ratio - 1.0) < 0.02, f"parity broken: accumulated/full-batch = {ratio:.4f} (expected ~1.0)"
options.rationale: Gradients are linear: backpropagating `loss / accum_steps` for each of the four micro-batches deposits the gradient of (mean₁ + mean₂ + mean₃ + mean₄) / 4 into `.grad` — and with equal chunks that is *exactly* the mean over all 256 rows, so parity holds to floating-point precision. The window structure stays intact: gradients accumulate across all four backward calls, then one `step()` consumes one large-batch-equivalent gradient and one `zero_grad()` opens the next window. Every downstream consumer — clipping, logging, the optimizer — now sees correctly scaled values.

hints: `MSELoss` with mean reduction averages *within* whatever batch it is handed, while `backward()` *adds* across calls. What does the sum of four per-micro-batch means equal, compared to one mean taken over all 256 rows at once?

Lines 18–19: each micro-batch deposits the gradient of an un-scaled mean loss into `.grad`, and four deposits later the buffer holds four full-batch gradients stacked on top of each other. Something in that loop has to carry the 1/accum_steps factor — and it isn't the optimizer.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`nn.MSELoss` defaults to mean reduction, so each micro-batch loss is averaged over its own 64 rows — not over the 256 rows of the window. `backward()` then *accumulates*: each call adds its gradient into `.grad`. The sum of four per-micro-batch means is four times the mean over all 256 rows (equal chunks make this exact), so after one window `.grad` holds precisely 4× the full-batch gradient — hence the eerily round 4.0000 the harness prints. In the production trainer every `optimizer.step()` therefore applies a 4× update: same lr on paper, 4× effective learning rate in practice, and the run diverges where the large-batch baseline converges.

### Why the fix is correct
Gradients are linear, so Σₖ ∇(Lₖ / K) = ∇((1/K) Σₖ Lₖ) — backpropagating each micro-batch loss divided by `accum_steps` makes the four accumulated contributions sum to exactly the gradient of the global mean. That restores the accumulation contract: K micro-batches ≡ one batch of K·m rows, verified here at ratio 1.0000. The window rhythm is the other half of the contract — accumulate across all K backward calls, then `step()` and `zero_grad()` once per window — and the fix scales the *gradient itself* rather than compensating in the optimizer, so clipping, logging, and any future optimizer all see honest values.
recruiterReview: Correct, and you fixed it at the right layer. The core insight is that mean reduction is per-micro-batch while backward() sums across them, so the 1/accum_steps factor has to travel with the loss — compensating in the learning rate would have left every gradient consumer (clipping, norm logging, a future Adam swap) staring at 4× values, and I'm glad you didn't take that bait. The parity harness asserting on gradients rather than loss curves is exactly how this class of bug should be pinned down. One hardening note before this ships: `X.chunk` happens to split evenly here, but a ragged final micro-batch breaks the equal-weights assumption — scale by `len(xb) / window_size` (or use sum reduction divided by total window rows) so the contract survives any dataset length. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Gradient Accumulation: Big-Batch Math on Small-Batch Memory

Gradient accumulation is the standard trick for training with an effective batch size your GPU cannot hold: run K small forward/backward passes, let the gradients pile up in `.grad`, and call `optimizer.step()` once per window of K micro-batches. It works because of two facts. First, PyTorch's `backward()` deliberately *accumulates* — `param.grad += new_grad` — so consecutive backward calls naturally sum their contributions. Second, gradients are linear: the gradient of a sum of losses equals the sum of their gradients. Stack K micro-batch gradients and you have, in principle, the gradient of one big batch.

### The scaling contract
The subtlety is the word *mean*. Nearly every loss (`MSELoss`, `CrossEntropyLoss`) defaults to mean reduction over the batch it is handed. A micro-batch loss is therefore averaged over m rows, not over the K·m rows of the window — and the sum of K per-micro-batch means is K times the mean over the whole window. Accumulate un-scaled losses and `.grad` ends up holding K× the large-batch gradient, which is indistinguishable from multiplying your learning rate by K. The fix is to make each backward pass carry its share of the window: `(loss / accum_steps).backward()`. With equal micro-batches the accumulated gradient then matches the full-batch gradient exactly, to floating-point precision.

### The window rhythm
A correct accumulation loop has a strict cadence: `zero_grad()` once at the start of the window, then K rounds of forward + scaled backward, then (optionally) gradient clipping, then `step()` — once. Zeroing inside the micro-batch loop silently throws away all but the last micro-batch; stepping inside it gives you K tiny updates of a 1/K-scaled gradient, which crawls. Schedulers belong on the optimizer-step cadence too: stepping a warmup schedule per micro-batch finishes warmup K× too early.

### Why this bug ships so easily
Nothing throws. With vanilla SGD the run simply behaves like its learning rate is K× larger — sometimes it diverges loudly, sometimes it just trains worse. With Adam the symptom is muted because Adam's update is roughly scale-invariant in the gradient, so teams can carry the bug for months until someone enables norm-based gradient clipping (which now clamps against inflated norms) or compares against a true large-batch baseline. The reliable detector is a parity harness: identical weights, one full-batch backward as reference, one accumulation window as the trainer runs it, assert the gradients match. Assert on gradients, not loss curves — gradients are where the contract lives. One honest caveat: layers that compute batch statistics (BatchNorm) see m rows, not K·m, so accumulation is only *exactly* equivalent for stat-free models; that residual gap is expected and small, the K× scale error is neither.

### Common variations of this bug
- Forgetting the 1/K scale entirely (this challenge) — effective learning rate multiplied by K.
- A ragged final micro-batch when the dataset size is not divisible by K, so equal 1/K weights no longer reproduce the global mean — weight by micro-batch size instead.
- `zero_grad()` placed inside the micro-batch loop, silently shrinking the effective batch to one micro-batch with K−1 wasted passes.
- `optimizer.step()` called every micro-batch while the loss is still divided by K — updates K× too small, training crawls and the LR schedule desynchronizes.
- Logging the scaled `loss / K` value, making training curves look K× better than the model actually is.
- Gradient clipping applied with thresholds tuned on un-accumulated runs, clamping against norms that are K× inflated.
- BatchNorm models compared against a true large-batch baseline and expected to match exactly — batch statistics break exact parity even when the scaling is right.
